title: Fever with flank pain and dysuria

chart.oneLiner: 35F presents with 2 days of fever, chills, right flank pain, and urinary frequency and burning, with nausea and vomiting.

opening: I've had a high fever and chills, pain in my back on the right, and it burns when I pee.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 35-year-old woman, febrile, uncomfortable. Good historian.

HPI: 2 days of fever, rigors, right flank/back pain, urinary frequency, urgency, and dysuria; nausea with a couple episodes of vomiting. Started as lower urinary symptoms that ascended. NO vaginal discharge, NO recent instrumentation, NO known kidney stones (only if asked). Last menstrual period normal (if asked).
PMH: prior uncomplicated UTIs. MEDS: none. SOCIAL: sexually active.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: febrile, mildly ill-appearing.
- Vitals: BP 110/68, HR 104, RR 18, 38.9°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Abdomen: suprapubic tenderness; RIGHT costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness.
- No peritonism. Cardiac/lungs: normal.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- CVA tenderness is revealed only if the back/flanks are examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.UA: Cloudy; large leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, many WBCs and bacteria; WBC casts.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline; ventricular rate ~110.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline; ventricular rate ~110. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: No consolidation (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the fever source).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal PA chest film.
cxr.imageDescription: No consolidation (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the fever source).

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute pyelonephritis (leading,8). Lower UTI / cystitis (core,3). Obstructing stone with infection (CANT-MISS,4). Pelvic inflammatory disease (core,3). Appendicitis (CANT-MISS to consider,2). Nephrolithiasis (core,2). Names pyelonephritis with supporting features — fever + CVA tenderness + pyuria (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute pyelonephritis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Obstructing stone with infection; Appendicitis. Also reasonable: Lower UTI / cystitis; Pelvic inflammatory disease; Nephrolithiasis.

coreActions.item: Names pyelonephritis with supporting features — fever + CVA tenderness + pyuria

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Urinalysis (CANT-MISS,4) and urine culture (3). CBC (2). BMP (1). Lactate (1). Blood cultures if systemically ill (2). Pregnancy test in a woman of childbearing age (1). Imaging (CT/US) if obstruction/stone suspected or not improving (1). PENALTY: shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); no acute ischemic changes (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia (-3).
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.
commonMistakes: calling it sinus when the rhythm is irregularly irregular
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (rate ~110); irregularly irregular, no discernible P waves.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no consolidation. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes it excludes pneumonia as the fever source (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes it excludes pneumonia as the fever source
criticalFindings: No consolidation (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the fever source).
steps.idealAnswer: No consolidation (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the fever source).

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: No consolidation (obtained to exclude pneumonia as the fever source).

coreActions.item: notes it excludes pneumonia as the fever source

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Pyuria with WBC casts, nitrites, leukocytosis, and CVA tenderness. What is your diagnosis?
steps.rubric: Acute pyelonephritis (6). Cites upper-tract features — CVA tenderness, systemic illness, WBC casts (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Obtain urine (and blood) cultures, then empiric antibiotics covering gram-negatives — e.g., ceftriaxone or a fluoroquinolone per local resistance (CANT-MISS,8). IV fluids and antipyretics/analgesia (5). Disposition — admit if septic, pregnant, vomiting/unable to tolerate oral, or complicated; otherwise outpatient (5). Image for obstruction/stone if not improving in 48–72h (CANT-MISS to consider,4). Tailor antibiotics to culture (3). PENALTY: missing an obstructed/infected system needing urgent drainage (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Plan: ceftriaxone or a fluoroquinolone per local resistance; Image for obstruction/stone if not improving in 48–72h.

Image for obstruction/stone if not improving in 48–72h

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 35F presents with 2 days of fever, chills, right flank pain, and urinary frequency and burning, with nausea and vomiting. The diagnosis is Pyelonephritis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: suprapubic tenderness; RIGHT costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Pyelonephritis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Pyelonephritis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
